data.title: Corning plans Springboard upgrade through 2030 while forecasting Q2 sales about $4.6B and EPS $0.73-$0.77
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Corning Incorporated (GLW) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “Year-over-year, sales grew 18% to $4.35 billion. EPS grew 30% to $0.70. Operating margin expanded 220 basis points to 20.2%.” (Chairman, President & CEO Wendell Weeks)

  • “Now based on increasing demand for our innovations, we actually plan to upgrade again and extend our plan through 2030 at our investor event in New York City on May 6.” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks)

  • “We now have concluded two more large, long-term agreements with hyperscale customers, and they are each similar in size and duration to the Meta agreement.” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks)

  • “Our ramp is running behind our ambitious plans. Our wafer facility will undergo an extended maintenance shutdown, and we will transition to a permanent power system and repair and upgrade production equipment to increase throughput in future quarters.” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks)

  • “We delivered our eighth consecutive quarter of year-over-year sales growth while continuing to enhance the financial profile of the company.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Edward Schlesinger)

  • “Today, we announced changes to our segment reporting effective first quarter 2026, which better align with our current operating and management structure.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

Outlook

  • “In the second quarter, we expect to grow sales about 14% year-over-year to approximately $4.6 billion and to grow EPS about 25% year-over-year to a range of $0.73 to $0.77.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • “Our second quarter forecast includes an additional $30 million of expense in Q2 versus Q1 as our solar wafer plant undergoes an extended maintenance shutdown.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • “For the full year, we expect to generate significantly more free cash flow year-over-year while continuing to invest strongly in our growth vectors aided by customer financial support.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • The provided analysts’ estimates data could not be validated to the required fiscal-quarter format described in the prompt, so no estimates comparisons are included.

Financial results

  • “Year-over-year, in Q1, sales grew 18% to $4.35 billion and EPS increased 30% to $0.70 per share, both coming in at the high point of our guidance.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • “In Optical Communications, sales were $1.8 billion, up 36% year-over-year, driven by robust demand for Gen AI products. Net income was $387 million, up 93% year-over-year.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • “In Solar, sales were $370 million, up $164 million or 80% year-over-year. Net income was $7 million, down $20 million year-over-year.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • “Our first quarter actuals included about a $0.04 EPS impact as we continue to bring up solar wafer capacity to meet committed demand.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • “In the quarter, OpEx was $823 million. Included in Q1 OpEx was higher variable compensation expense, including stock-based compensation.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

Q&A

  • John Roberts, Mizuho: “On the new hyperscaler agreements, are there material glass fiber draw capacity expansions associated with that?” Chairman, President & CEO Weeks: “You’re going to see expansion across all of our major Optical operations, including expanding our fiber operations.”

  • John Roberts, Mizuho: “When you complete and you're fully ramped on solar, what would be the approximate breakdown between semiconductor wafers and modules?” Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger: “We’re running at about $0.5 billion semiconductor business.” Chairman, President & CEO Weeks: “Wafer and module. Both of those.”

  • Wamsi Mohan, BofA: “How tight are you seeing the current state? Are you able to meet ... demand? And how are you seeing the evolution of pricing?” Chairman, President & CEO Weeks: “The pricing environment is clearly favorable for those who have capacity.”

  • Wamsi Mohan, BofA: “Are we entering an environment in Optical where you could eclipse ... margins greater than display?” Chairman, President & CEO Weeks: “So the simple answer is, yes.”

  • Joshua Spector, UBS: “Do you expect to have a new margin target that you're going to put out there next week?” Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger: “Hopefully, you’ll tune in ... and we can talk about all the impacts of our financial profile.”

  • Asiya Merchant, Citi: “Are you able to kind of within these contracts, raise prices over the long term?” Chairman, President & CEO Weeks: “What we’re mainly focused on here is improving visibility.”

  • Asiya Merchant, Citi: “When should we expect the drag on these expenses to be completed...?” Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger: “When you take that in aggregate, it’s probably close to $0.07 of EPS in the second quarter guide that we gave.”

  • Samik Chatterjee, JPMorgan: “How should we think about what that actually implies?” Chairman, President & CEO Weeks: “The simple answer is yes, all of the above.”

  • Meta Marshall, Morgan Stanley: “Are you guys starting to see some of the BEAD demand?” Chairman, President & CEO Weeks: “BEAD is still quite small.”

  • Brendan Rogers, for Wolfe: “Can you guys share any more details on kind of the split between Carrier and Enterprise growth rates?” Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger: “Both Carrier and Enterprise grew 36% year-over-year in Q1.”

  • Martin Yang, Oppenheimer: “You haven't raised the CapEx plan despite the two new agreements.” Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger: “CapEx would be about $1.7 billion. We could be a little above that number this year.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly skeptical and detail-seeking on capacity, pricing, and contract structure, including “sharing the risk” (JPMorgan) and whether the market is seeing “price increases” (BofA).

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and confident on optical demand and profitability, including “the simple answer is, yes” on optical margin potential (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks), while more cautious on solar wafer timing, including “calling the exact timing ... is very hard to do” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management shifted from describing deals “in the process of concluding” to “concluded two more” hyperscaler agreements (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks), and from a 2028-focused Springboard upgrade to “upgrade again and extend ... through 2030” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Strategic scope expanded from “add $11 billion in incremental annualized sales by the end of 2028” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks, Q4 call) to “upgrade again and extend our plan through 2030” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks, Q1 call).

  • Customer-contract momentum in optical advanced from “We are concluding similar long-term agreements with other major customers” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks, Q4 call) to “We now have concluded two more large, long-term agreements with hyperscale customers” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks, Q1 call).

  • Solar narrative shifted from ramp costs being “the primary drag on net income” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger, Q4 call) to a specific operational disruption and cost, including “an extended maintenance shutdown” and “$30 million of additional expense versus the first quarter” in Q2 guidance (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks).

Risks and concerns

  • “Our ramp is running behind our ambitious plans.” (Chairman, President & CEO Weeks)

  • “It impacts our margin, it impacts our EPS and it also reduces our sales because we’re shut down for a period of time here.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

  • “We expect memory prices to significantly impact the market in 2026.” (Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Schlesinger)

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a Springboard “proof point” driven by Optical Communications and Solar, highlighted two additional hyperscaler long-term agreements similar to the Meta deal, and guided Q2 to about $4.6 billion in sales and $0.73 to $0.77 EPS while absorbing $30 million of incremental solar wafer expense tied to an extended shutdown aimed at improving future throughput. The company also said it will present an upgraded and extended Springboard plan through 2030 at its May 6 investor event, alongside more detail on Gen AI and a new Photonics market-access platform.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: IAC changes name, shifting focus to publishing and MGM Resort investment
data.description:
IAC (IAC) is changing its name to People Incorporated amid a focus on its publishing business and investment in MGM Resorts International (MGM).

In a letter to shareholders, IAC Chairman Barry Diller announced the name change and the company’s history, which evolved from Silver King Communications to IAC/InterActiveCorp, a conglomerate of more than 200 companies and 100 minority investments, including a 26% investment in MGM Resorts (MGM).

With the name change, the company will transition the “necessary” staff of IAC into People Incorporated and “significantly reduce overhead” to concentrate on the two assets,” People publishing and holdings in MGM Resorts (MGM).

While Diller did not directly address any reduction in workforce, he intimated as such given a downsizing to a smaller company.

“The corpus of People Incorporated will include the assets of a mostly virtual media business together with the very hard assets of MGM Resorts—if you like, a perfect hedge in a world that is changing so unpredictably fast,” Diller said, adding that he will remain as chairman and senior executive of the new company.

The name change is expected to occur by the company's second quarter earnings in August.

data.title: Two S&P 500 support levels to watch
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) (SPY) (VOO) is consolidating near all-time highs following a sharp rebound from its March lows, but the index’s next test may hinge more on defending gains than reaching new peaks.

Liz Thomas, head of investment strategy at SoFi, on Tuesday identified two critical technical support zones to watch if the rally starts to cool. 

The first sits around 6,975 to 7,000, where a 23.6% Fibonacci retracement coincides with a psychologically significant round number and former resistance level. This zone will determine whether the market can successfully turn an old ceiling into a new floor. SP500 was changing hands at 7,136 at press time.

The deeper support zone lies between 6,780 and 6,850, aligning the 38.2% retracement with the 50-day moving average—a level that has proven significant repeatedly over the past seven months, Thomas noted.

data.title: S&P 500 hides weakness as equal-weight index looks to log a 6-day slide
data.description:
U.S. equities are showing signs of underlying weakness despite the broader market’s seemingly stable performance, as a divergence in index structure points to a more fragile backdrop.

The equal-weight S&P 500 (SP500), which offers a clearer view of average stock performance, is on track for its sixth straight daily decline—marking its longest losing streak since December 2024.

This downturn suggests that selling pressure is becoming more widespread beneath the surface, even as headline indexes remain relatively resilient. Since the market’s rebound from its March 30 low, gains have appeared broadly distributed in absolute terms. However, a closer look reveals that much of the upward momentum has been driven by a smaller group of large-cap names. 

The traditional cap-weighted S&P 500 continues to outperform, benefiting disproportionately from strength in mega-cap stocks. Meanwhile, the equal-weighted version still trades below its January peak, underscoring the uneven nature of the recovery. 

Together, these dynamics highlight a hidden correction, where internal market breadth deteriorates even as top-line index performance masks the extent of underlying weakness.

S&P 500 Funds: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (FXAIX), (VFIAX), (VFFSX), and (SWPPX).

data.title: Amgen, Astra launch real-time trials as FDA to expedite drug development using AI
data.description:
The U.S. FDA announced on Tuesday that Amgen (AMGN) and AstraZeneca (AZN) have initiated real-time clinical trials as the agency seeks to launch a pilot program designed to accelerate drug development using AI and data science.

In a statement, the agency added that it is requesting public information to introduce the pilot program this summer based on the learnings from the proofs-of-concept studies targeting cancer therapies.

Typically, study data from early-stage clinical trials are collected by trial sites initially before being sent to regulators through study sponsors, a process that can take years. “The lag time can delay regulatory decisions unnecessarily and slow down the drug development timeline,” remarked FDA Commissioner Marty Makary.

“With improvements in AI and data science, sponsors and trial sites have the opportunity to conduct real-time trials in a way that enhances safety monitoring and radically increases efficiency,” the FDA said.

Astra’s (AZN) Phase 2 trial, TRAVERSE, conducted in collaboration with the University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center and the University of Pennsylvania, is testing its anticancer drug acalabrutinib, branded as Calquence, as a combination therapy for treatment-naïve mantle cell lymphoma.

Amgen (AMGN) is currently selecting sites for its Phase 1b trial, STREAM-SCLC, targeted at patients with limited-stage small cell lung carcinoma.

Both proof-of-concept clinical trials are designed to report critical findings, such as endpoint data, to the FDA in real time.

“This will help us accelerate promising therapies and build toward our ultimate goal of running real-time, continuous trials across all phases of drug development," Makary added.

The agency said it is accepting comments on the Request for Information until May 29 before completing pilot selections in August.

data.title: GM outlines $13.5B-$15.5B 2026 EBIT-adjusted outlook amid Iran-related cost uncertainty
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: General Motors (GM) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Once again, thanks to our strategic product portfolio and great execution by the GM team, including our dealers and suppliers, we delivered an outstanding quarter," said Chairman & CEO Mary Barra.
  • Barra said GM is "continuing to execute our plan to return to 8% to 10% EBIT-adjusted margins in North America for the full year," adding that in Q1 GM posted "an EBIT adjusted margin of 10.1%, including 1.5 points of benefit from the accounting adjustment resulting from the recent Supreme Court tariff decision. This nets to an 8.6% margin."
  • Barra highlighted U.S. mix and share positioning, including: "We also continue to lead in full-size pickup sales and share with 42% of the U.S. market," and said EV share improved, "As we exited the quarter, our EV market share in the U.S. was 13%, up from about 10% in December 2025."
  • Barra emphasized software and autonomy milestones, saying GM is "on pace to add more than 1 million OnStar subscribers in 2026" and that Super Cruise customers "have now driven 1 billion hands-free miles"; she also said, "We recently took the next step and began supervised on-road testing in California and Michigan" for eyes-off, hands-off technology targeted to "launching in 2028 on the Cadillac Escalade IQ."
  • Executive VP & CFO Paul Jacobson said, "Q1 EBIT adjusted was $4.3 billion," and added that performance came "even after excluding the $0.5 billion tariff adjustment."

Outlook

  • Jacobson raised full-year guidance, stating, "we are raising our overall EBIT-adjusted guidance to $13.5 billion to $15.5 billion, up from $13 billion to $15 billion" and "raising our EPS diluted adjusted guidance to $11.50 to $13.50 per share, up from $11 to $13."
  • Management tied caution to geopolitics. Barra said, "the war in Iran has raised our costs and its duration remains uncertain," and added, "we believe it's prudent to wait and see how events unfold before we make any further changes to guidance."
  • On cost assumptions, Jacobson said commodity inflation (including logistics and DRAM) is now expected at "$1.5 billion to $2 billion," while "gross tariff costs are now expected to be $2.5 billion to $3.5 billion for the year" (revised from "$3 billion to $4 billion"), reflecting the Q1 tariff accounting adjustment.
  • EV planning remained centered on lower volume. Jacobson said GM expects EV volumes "to be lower as the market shows early signs of stabilizing around 6% of U.S. industry sales," while maintaining North America price assumptions: "we continue to expect to be flat, up 0.5%."

Financial results

  • Jacobson reported Q1 EBIT-adjusted of "$4.3 billion" and said total company revenue was "down year-over-year by approximately $400 million" driven by "lower EV wholesale volumes."
  • Jacobson detailed Q1 tariff and warranty dynamics, saying, "we incurred $200 million of incremental gross tariff costs," and on warranty, "we continue to expect a year-over-year tailwind of $1 billion with first quarter results improving roughly $200 million versus the prior year."
  • Jacobson disclosed additional EV restructuring charges: "In the first quarter, we took an additional $1.1 billion in EV charges" and said, "we expect about $1 billion of this will have a future cash impact." He added, "we've already recorded around 90% of the expected total supplier commercial claim costs," and that "most of the remaining cash flow" from EV-related cash charges is expected "to occur in 2026."
  • GM Financial performance and cash returns were highlighted as stable: Jacobson said, "GM Financial continued its stable performance, delivering EBT adjusted of $700 million," and also noted capital returns, including "$800 million in open market stock repurchases" and "$19 billion of cash" with "$5.5 billion remaining" on repurchase authorization.
  • Digital services disclosure increased. Jacobson said, "In Q1, we saw recognized revenue of over $750 million, up over 20% year-over-year," and guided, "for the calendar year, we expect $3.1 billion of recognized revenue, up 15% year-over-year." He added deferred revenue ended Q1 at "$5.8 billion" and is expected to "approach $7.5 billion" for 2026.

Q&A

  • Itay Michaeli, TD Cowen: asked about offsets to higher commodity inflation and SDV ARPU; Executive VP & CFO Jacobson said GM will be "measured" on cost actions and that with SDV 2.0 "the number of opportunities out there starts to magnify," adding, "we already have significantly higher volumes, higher deferred revenue, more realized revenue."
  • Joseph Spak, UBS: pressed on discounting, pricing leeway, and IEEPA mechanics; Jacobson said share pressures were tied to inventory and GM aims to reverse share without "heavy discounting," while clarifying, "all we've done here is taken the IEEPA direct tariff that we paid last year...and credited that back as a receivable" and said GM did not change free cash flow guidance due to refund timing uncertainty.
  • Emmanuel Rosner, Wolfe: asked what could move guidance; Barra said the key variable is "what happens from -- with the Iranian conflict" because "oil prices affect a lot more" and added GM has a portfolio to manage potential mix shifts, noting, "to date, we really haven't seen" mix impacts.
  • Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs: asked about additional pickup downtime and Super Cruise demand breadth; Jacobson said GM is "not anticipating any material downtime," and said Super Cruise renewals are "about that 40% attachment rate" after the prepaid period.
  • James Picariello, BNP Paribas: asked about GM Financial dividends and EV cash charges timing; Jacobson said GMF dividend timing stepped up in Q1 but "not changing the full year expectation," and added GM expects "substantially all of that cash paid out before the end" of Q2 for most supplier commercial claims.
  • Ryan Brinkman, JPMorgan: asked about China run-rate and exports; Barra said, "we continue to be one of the only, if not one of the only Western OEMs that is profitable and growing share" in China, but added, "the China market is seeing some weakness" and she was "not in a position" to project equity income.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts sentiment was slightly negative, centered on uncertainty and pressure-testing guidance around "the Iranian conflict," cost inflation, discounting, and China durability.
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks (emphasizing "outstanding quarter" and raised guidance) and more cautious in Q&A when discussing Iran-driven oil/commodity sensitivity and the decision to "wait and see" before further guidance changes.
  • Versus last quarter’s tone around adapting to tax/trade policy and EV-demand reset, this call added more event-driven caution on Iran-related costs while keeping confidence in execution and capital return priorities.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance increased from Q4’s "$13 billion to $15 billion" EBIT-adjusted and "$11 to $13" EPS to Q1’s "$13.5 billion to $15.5 billion" and "$11.50 to $13.50" EPS, with management explicitly linking the uplift to "the tariff adjustment" while flagging Iran-driven uncertainty.
  • Digital services disclosure expanded from Q4’s emphasis on deferred revenue expectations to Q1’s explicit quarterly recognized revenue ("over $750 million") and deferred revenue balance ("$5.8 billion"), alongside subscriber and ARPU framing.
  • EV restructuring progressed from Q4’s expectation of "significantly smaller" future charges to Q1’s additional "$1.1 billion" charge and a timeline to resolve "most" supplier agreements in Q2.

Risks and concerns

  • Barra said the "war in Iran has raised our costs" and management repeatedly tied inflation assumptions to sustained oil/commodity levels, with Jacobson describing guidance as based on the "current environment kind of persisting for the year."
  • Jacobson flagged higher cost pressures and uncertainty, including "incremental commodity and freight costs" and gross tariffs still expected at "$2.5 billion to $3.5 billion" for 2026.
  • Inventory constraints remained a near-term operational risk, with Jacobson saying, "leaner inventory constrained retail sales" and that GM will increase inventory "over the next several quarters" while staying mindful of demand.

Final takeaway

GM management framed Q1 as a margin-strong start in North America and paired it with higher full-year EBIT-adjusted and EPS guidance primarily reflecting a tariff accounting adjustment, while repeatedly underscoring that Iran-driven oil and commodity volatility is the largest swing factor. Executives emphasized disciplined incentives and inventory, accelerated disclosure and scaling of OnStar/Super Cruise economics, and an effort to close out EV-related supplier claims quickly so they do not remain a cash-flow overhang in 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Puig says no decision has been made on deal with Estée Lauder
data.description:
Puig (PUIGF) said no final decision has been made about its potential deal with Estée Lauder (EL).

"Unless and until an agreement is reached, there can be no assurances regarding the transaction or the terms," Puig said in a statement with its Q1 results on Monday.

The update comes after Estée Lauder (EL) and Spanish beauty group Puig (PUIGF),confirmed that the companies were in talks about a combination in March. They didn't disclose any terms of a potential combination.

A merger between Estée Lauder (EL) and Puig (PUIGF) could create one of the world’s largest luxury beauty groups, with a combined enterprise value around $40B and annual sales near $20B. The combination would bring together Estée Lauder's (EL) scale in skincare and makeup with Puig’s strength in fragrance and brand-building, including names like Carolina Herrera, Rabanne, Jean Paul Gaultier, Byredo, MAC, Charlotte Tilbury, and Le Labo.

data.title: Lululemon moves to block founder's board nominees; called out for working with rivals
data.description:
Lululemon athletica (LULU) is advising shareholders to vote for its current slate of board members and reject those nominated by founder and former CEO Chip Wilson in a proxy statement filed ahead of the company’s annual meeting of shareholders.

“The board of directors does not endorse any of Mr. Wilson’s nominees,” the statement says, adding that if any shareholder has already completed and signed a proxy card provided by, or on behalf of, Wilson, they have “every legal right” to change their vote.

Lululemon (LULU) is fighting Wilson’s attempt to replace three board members to “catalyze a quantum of change” that Wilson claims is “sorely needed at lululemon.”

Wilson argues that the spirit of the company he founded in the late 1990s has been compromised by corporate greed, a lack of vision, and a shift toward mediocrity, all of which he pins on the ineffectual board.

“Like a plane crash, declines rarely happen because of a single failure. It’s a series of mistakes,” Wilson said in a letter to The Wall Street Journal, pointing to lululemon’s (LULU) 62% loss in share value over the past three years.

Wilson is looking to oust board members associated with private equity firm Advent, including David Mussafer.

To preclude any attempts by Wilson to change the current dynamic of the lululemon (LULU) board, the company provided background on his tenure and departure at the company, including accusations that Wilson’s wife and son had formed a competing company while Wilson was still on the lululemon (LULU) board.

Wilson was also accused of publicly airing personal grievances against the board in a book, violating a support agreement by criticizing the company in media interviews, and more recently of providing competitors Alo and Vuori with “his playbook.”

As recently as two weeks ago, Wilson created a new venture in the technical apparel space.

Lululemon is expected to hold their annual shareholder meeting on June 11, 2026.

data.title: Incyte outlines $4.77B-$4.94B 2026 net sales while preparing four launches over 12 months
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Incyte (INCY) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO William Meury framed Q1 as execution toward a post-Jakafi portfolio, saying, "We're off to a strong start in 2026 with net sales up 20% year-over-year, driven by strong demand across our entire portfolio," and adding, "We view '26 as a year of strategic progress as we transition Incyte beyond a single cornerstone product toward a high-quality growth-oriented portfolio across Hematology, Oncology and Immunology."

  • Meury highlighted near-term regulatory and launch catalysts, including, "the FDA accepted our regulatory application for povorcitinib in patients with moderate to severe HS," and said the company "remain[s] on track for several regulatory decisions this year, including Jakafi XR" and "Opzelura for moderate atopic dermatitis in Europe."

  • Meury detailed leadership changes and commercial reorganization: "we announced the appointment of Suky Upadhyay as Chief Financial Officer" and also announced "Pablo Cagnoni as President Incyte and Global Head of Research and Development; and Steven Stein as Executive Vice President, Chief Medical Officer and Head of Late-Stage Development," while saying the U.S. commercial integration "is intended to establish consistent standards and enterprise-level capabilities... creating a launch-ready organization in 2026."

  • Quote (Principal financial officer, VP of Finance & Chief Accounting Officer Thomas Tray): "We reaffirm our full year 2026 total net sales, R&D and SG&A operating expense guidance."

Outlook

  • Tray reaffirmed full-year 2026 guidance: "Total net sales guidance for the full year 2026 is $4.77 billion to $4.94 billion" and said it "includes net sales expectations for Jakafi of $3.22 billion to $3.27 billion, Opzelura of $750 million to $790 million and Hematology and Oncology products of $800 million to $880 million."

  • Management’s near-term growth plan continued to center on lifecycle and new launches: Meury said, "This business will be supported by 4 new product launches over the next 12 months, including Jakafi XR, Opzelura for moderate AD in Europe, Monjuvi in first-line DLBCL and povorcitinib in HS," and added, "We anticipate the approval and launch of XR in the middle of the year."

  • Versus the prior quarter, the company maintained the same full-year sales and expense ranges, while Q1 commentary added new regulatory progress (FDA acceptance for povorcitinib in HS) and highlighted a new executive slate and a consolidated U.S. commercial structure.

Financial Results

  • In Q1, Meury reported, "Total revenue in the first quarter of '26 was $1.27 billion," and said net sales were "$1.1 billion."

  • Jakafi performance remained the largest contributor, with Meury stating, "Jakafi sales in the first quarter were $758 million," alongside prescription demand growth and launch preparation, including, "We anticipate the approval and launch of XR in the middle of the year."

  • Key non-Jakafi product sales and traction were updated: Meury said "Opzelura... sales of $143 million" and noted hematology/oncology growth with "net sales grew 116% to $204 million," while providing individual product prints including "Niktimvo... $55 million," "Monjuvi sales were $49 million" and "Zynyz sales were $41 million."

  • Expense and margin commentary emphasized leverage: Tray said, "Our GAAP R&D expenses were $516 million" and "GAAP SG&A expenses were $328 million," while adding, "Ongoing operating expenses for the first quarter of 2026 increased 14% year-over-year compared to a 19% increase in ongoing revenues... leading to a continued increase in operating leverage and margins."

Q&A

  • Tazeen Ahmad, BofA: asked how povorcitinib in vitiligo would coexist commercially with Opzelura; CEO Meury responded, "there is a natural sequencing that sets up in the vitiligo market, and we're able to cover sort of the waterfront with both Opzelura as well as with povorcitinib," and EVP Mohamed Issa said, "for patients with BSA greater than 10... we estimate that total addressable market to be about $1.5 billion to $2 billion."

  • James Shin, Deutsche Bank: asked whether the upcoming 989 update is incremental and what a new CFO changes; President Pablo Cagnoni answered, "the update at EHA, it's going to be pretty substantial," and described expected enrollment levels, while CEO Meury said the CFO focus includes, "capital allocation decisions" and ensuring "your budget planning process is efficient and sharp."

  • Etzer Darout, Barclays: asked about an accelerated Niktimvo first-line chronic GVHD combo-with-rux timeline; Cagnoni said the combo study "accrued very quickly" and "we'll have data before the end of this year" to inform regulatory strategy.

  • Matthew Phipps, William Blair: asked about a temporary pause related to pneumonitis events in the KRAS G12D program; Cagnoni said, "The Phase III study was never put in pause," and characterized the Europe Phase I hold as "an administrative reason."

  • Judah Frommer, Morgan Stanley: asked about Opzelura competition and whether povorcitinib matters to the long-term Opzelura trajectory; CEO Meury cited U.S. new-start share and said, "In the first quarter, our share of new patient starts in the United States was 46%," while describing growth components that include potential Europe moderate AD contribution.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly positive, with multiple questions focused on commercial durability (Opzelura competition, vitiligo segmentation), program risk controls (pneumonitis review), and regulatory/endpoint flexibility (989 ET/MF design), indicating detailed diligence rather than broad skepticism.

  • Management sentiment was positive in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, using confidence language around execution and differentiation, including Cagnoni’s, "we remain confident in the underlying thesis" on JAK2 V617F, and his assertion that the EHA 989 update will be "pretty substantial."

  • Compared with the prior quarter, Q1 carried a more execution-and-catalyst tone (FDA acceptance, new Phase III initiations, management appointments), while Q4 emphasized year-end performance recap and 2026 setup.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 introduced a management reshuffle and U.S. commercial integration that were not described as completed in Q4, including Meury’s announcement of a new CFO and a "single organization" for U.S. commercial operations.

  • Q1 added new pipeline/regulatory specifics versus Q4, including the FDA’s acceptance of povorcitinib in HS and positive Phase III vitiligo results supporting a filing timeline, while keeping the same full-year 2026 sales and expense guidance ranges reiterated previously.

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4’s broader launch planning and pipeline positioning toward Q1’s more technical trial-design and safety-administration questions (989 dosing mechanics and endpoints, 734 pneumonitis review, Niktimvo combo timing).

Risks and Concerns

  • Safety monitoring and enrollment optics emerged around 734, with Cagnoni referencing "4 cases of pneumonitis" in "more than 350 patients treated" and emphasizing conclusions of "no signal" after review.

  • Regulatory and endpoint uncertainty was a recurring theme for 989, as management repeatedly referenced ongoing and future FDA alignment on MF endpoints, with Cagnoni stating, "we need to spend a little bit more time with FDA discussing the design of the second-line MF study."

  • Launch execution risk was framed around access and sequencing, with Meury saying for Jakafi XR, "Our immediate focus will be on securing adequate formulary coverage for XR over the next 12 months post launch."

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q1 as the start of an execution-heavy year, pairing reaffirmed 2026 net sales guidance with a multi-launch cadence (Jakafi XR and Europe Opzelura near term, with Monjuvi first-line DLBCL and povorcitinib in HS positioned behind regulatory milestones). The call also elevated pipeline visibility—especially around 989’s pivotal path and 734’s late-stage build—while emphasizing leadership changes and a consolidated U.S. commercial organization intended to support portfolio-wide launches.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Zimmer Biomet raises 2026 adjusted EPS to $8.40-$8.55 while targeting over 200 robotics clinical hires by end of 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Zimmer Biomet (ZBH) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We're off to a strong start to the year strategically, operationally and financially" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Ivan Tornos), as the company reported "sales 2.9% on an organic constant currency basis" and "adjusted EPS of $2.09."
  • "U.S. knee growth of 2.2%" included "a greater than 20% increase in partial knee sales" tied to "our Oxford Partial Cementless Knee" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos), while "pressure in our legacy total knee implants such as NexGen and Vanguard" was attributed to "our brand rationalization strategy."
  • "Our technology and data, bone cement and surgical business grew nearly 12%" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos), and he said mBos feedback was "overwhelmingly positive" and that the company "continue[s] to expect U.S. approval and the launch of the semi-autonomous version in early 2027 followed by the fully autonomous version in late 2027 or early 2028."
  • "Dr. Jonathan Vigdorchik... has joined Zimmer Biomet as Chief Science, Technology and Medical Affairs Officer reporting to me" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos), alongside a CFO transition in which "Suky... decision to leave" was announced and "Paul Stellato... will serve as Interim Chief Financial Officer."
  • "Net sales were $2.087 billion" and "we delivered diluted earnings per share of $2.09" (CFO and Executive VP of Finance, Operations & Supply Chain Suketu Upadhyay), with "operating cash flows of $359 million and free cash flow of $246 million" and "$250 million of share repurchases in the first quarter."

Outlook

  • "We are maintaining our full year 2026 organic constant currency revenue growth guidance of 1% to 3%" and "our assumption of up to 100 basis points of price erosion is unchanged" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos).
  • "Our reported sales guidance also remains unchanged at 2.5% to 4.5%" and "we continue to assume up to $750 million of share repurchases" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos).
  • "We are raising both our EPS and free cash flow growth expectations" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos): "adjusted EPS to be $8.40 to $8.55" (from "$8.30 to $8.45" previously) and "free cash flow growth... 9% to 11%" (from "8% to 10%" previously).
  • "We now expect 2026 operating margins to be better than anticipated, down slightly less than 50 basis points from 2025" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos), and he guided cadence including "operating margins in the second quarter of 2026 being down roughly 200 basis points" year-over-year.

Financial Results

  • "We reported GAAP diluted earnings per share of $1.22" and "adjusted gross margin was 73%" (CFO and Executive VP Upadhyay), while "adjusted operating margin was 27.3%."
  • "Consolidated pricing was 40 basis points negative" and results included "opportunistic end-of-quarter purchases above historical levels" (CFO and Executive VP Upadhyay).
  • "Notably, the first quarter saw a $0.20 benefit from tariff-related items" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos), and the CFO added it was "largely driven by the invalidation of the IEEPA tariffs" with "a bit of a pull forward" from refunds.

Q&A

  • Frederick Wise, Stifel: "you didn't raise" revenue guidance despite the quarter; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos: "the word that comes to mind is confident" and "it's early in the year... This is a year of transition."
  • Vijay Kumar, Evercore ISI: asked for salesforce metrics and macro; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos: "roughly 66%... were 1099s" at the start of 2026 and "at the end of Q1... slightly below 60%" with turnover "in the single-digit range" and "no material supply disruptions" tied to the Middle East.
  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan: asked about one-time items; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos: "We lost 2 accounts" in U.S. knees and cited "a Kaiser strike" plus "disruption" in moving from "NexGen and Vanguard" to "Persona"; CFO and Executive VP Upadhyay: "we still expect" full-year gross margin "around 71%, give or take."
  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs: asked why OUS trailed; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos: "we made... some distributor changes" and said the company expects "international mid-single digit in the second half of 2026."
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: asked about Monogram rollout and ROSA; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos: "we continue to anticipate" launch "in early 2027" and said "the combination of Monogram plus ROSA plus TMINI is going to give us a competitive advantage."
  • Matthew Taylor, Jefferies: asked tariff assumptions; CFO and Executive VP Upadhyay: "we are assuming that the 122 tariffs remain intact" and "the IEEPA remain invalidated," while noting "the overall situation remains fluid."
  • Matthew Miksic, Barclays: asked on Paragon momentum and deals; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos: "we are in the teens" early in Q2 and "we're going to pause" on M&A while "continue to do buybacks."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly skeptical on guidance conservatism and sustainability, pressing on what was "onetime" and why guidance was not lifted more.
  • Management tone was slightly positive to confident in prepared remarks and repeated in Q&A, including "confident" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos) while emphasizing it is "early in the year" and the business is in "transition."
  • Versus the prior quarter’s emphasis on "tempered guidance" and disruption risk, Q1 language shifted to confidence plus specific proof points (salesforce conversion metrics and tariff-related timing).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance changed on profitability and cash but not sales: Q4 guided "adjusted EPS of $8.30 to $8.45" and Q1 raised to "$8.40 to $8.55" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos).
  • The U.S. sales channel narrative moved from explaining "the what, the why, the how and the when" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos, Q4) to reporting progress, including 1099 mix moving "slightly below 60%" and specialization "approaching... 30%."
  • Analysts’ focus stayed concentrated on the sales transition and durability of growth, but Q1 added more scrutiny on one-time tariff and knee-specific disruption drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Modest disruption" from U.S. commercial changes occurred and included "We lost 2 accounts" in U.S. knees (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos).
  • Tariff and policy uncertainty remained in view as "the overall situation remains fluid" (CFO and Executive VP Upadhyay).
  • Management change risk increased with "Suky's decision to leave" and an interim CFO appointment (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Tornos).

Final Takeaway

Zimmer Biomet’s management framed Q1 as a strong start, highlighted measurable progress in converting the U.S. salesforce to more dedicated and specialized coverage, and raised 2026 adjusted EPS and free cash flow growth expectations while keeping the sales growth outlook unchanged. The call also introduced a CFO transition to an interim leader, reaffirmed major technology launch timing for Monogram/mBos, and described knee-specific disruptions and tariff timing effects that influenced quarter results and guidance posture.
